metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or test to an owning child (75de or 5967) verifying that the completion-verifier code path does not invoke manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins().
evidence: Parent Settled Design: 'the completion-verifier branch does not parse plan-review-only phase, floor, or pins.'

Parent Signed plan-material pins section: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate...manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when absent...In the plan-review attestation branch, compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch...the completion-verifier branch does not parse plan-review-only phase, floor, or pins.'
5967 ACs: all relate to plan-review-branch behavior; none reference completion-verifier isolation.
75de ACs: cover 'every consumer' for drift/reuse/re-sign/audit but do not enumerate the completion-verifier as a verified-non-consumer.
No sibling has a test or AC asserting completion-verifier parser isolation.
findings.finding: The parent design specifies 'the completion-verifier branch does not parse plan-review-only phase, floor, or pins' as a deliberate isolation constraint. No child's acceptance criteria or test plan owns verification that the completion-verifier branch is NOT modified to call manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(). This is a named consumer of the manifest-parsing infrastructure whose isolation invariant is uncovered.
findings.location: No child — gap is absent from the full sibling roster
scenarios: A developer adding manifest_review_phase() or manifest_pins() calls to the completion-verifier branch (e.g., for debugging or symmetry) would not be caught by any existing child acceptance criterion.
A future reviewer running only the child test suites cannot confirm that the completion-verifier isolation is maintained after adding the new parsers.
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de or 5967, add an acceptance criterion: 'The completion-verifier branch does not call manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); a negative-path unit test confirms no ManifestFormatError is raised on a completion attestation containing unexpected phase/floor/pin lines.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named prerequisite coverage record schema co-located with the Pass 1 spec that closes the `reason_code` enum and maps each structural failure mode (missing, duplicate, schema-invalid, unknown-ID records) to a specific reason_code value.
evidence: Plan quote: 'indeterminate requires reason_code exactly evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid'
The plan also says 'Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, or schema-invalid records become indeterminate' but does not specify which reason_code these map to
The plan_review_verdict.schema.json has no prerequisite coverage record schema
ABSENCE: No section of the plan defines a prerequisite coverage schema object or a named reason_code enum — the three values appear only in the single prose sentence
findings.finding: The `indeterminate` coverage record's `reason_code` enum (evaluation-error, output-invalid, attribution-invalid) is defined inline in the prerequisite-consistency prose but is never co-located with the Pass 1 output schema. No schema definition names these values as a closed enum, so implementors of Pass 2/Pass 3 that branch on `reason_code` have no canonical reference.
findings.location: Prerequisite consistency review section: 'indeterminate requires reason_code exactly evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid plus optional diagnostic detail'
scenarios: Pass 3 maps indeterminate records to unsigned INDETERMINATE output — but which reason_code triggers which path (e.g. does 'output-invalid' suppress vs. does 'attribution-invalid')? The processing rule is missing.
Missing/duplicate/unknown-ID prerequisite records (per the plan: 'become indeterminate') have no specified reason_code assignment

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located processing rule in the manifest-phase section for the case where `priority-floor:` is present but `review-phase:` is absent, specifying whether this is treated as malformed, as planning (floor ignored), or as some other disposition.
evidence: Plan quote: 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent'
Plan quote: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80' — implying planning reviews do NOT carry a floor line
Plan quote: 'missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed' — but this only covers execution-phase manifests
ABSENCE: No rule specifies the validity outcome when priority-floor is present but review-phase is absent (or missing)
findings.finding: The plan says manifest_review_phase() returns 'planning' when 'the phase line is absent,' and separately that a planning-mode review is signed with review-phase: planning (no special case for omission). However the processing rule for a manifest that contains a priority-floor: line but no review-phase: line is unspecified — an implementor must guess whether this is malformed, treated as execution with planning mode, or treated as planning.
findings.location: Signed plan-material pins section: 'manifest_review_phase() returns planning only when the phase line is absent'; Execution reviews section: 'Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80'
scenarios: An older code path emits a manifest with priority-floor but no review-phase (e.g., partial migration or bug); the validity checker has no specified behavior
A floor value present without a phase line could be silently ignored or trigger malformed — the plan does not say which
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: a manifest carrying priority-floor: without review-phase: is treated as malformed (ManifestFormatError) since the floor is meaningless without a phase context, consistent with the 'missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed' rule already established for execution attestations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact sub-fields of `canonical target details` in DerivedPlanReviewHealth (e.g., `canonical_id`, `role`, `pinned_fingerprint`, `current_fingerprint`, and any status-per-pin list) so all five named surfaces can implement the same schema.
evidence: 'canonical target details' is listed alongside precisely defined peers (pin_status enum, phase_status enum, enforced boolean) but is itself undefined
AC 21: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract' — surfaces cannot consistently consume a contract whose target-detail fields are unspecified
findings.finding: The DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract lists 'canonical target details' as a field but never enumerates the sub-fields (e.g., canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint). An executor implementing the health output for five named surfaces — detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, web ticket-review UI — cannot determine what to render without knowing the exact field set.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout — 'compute_plan_review_validity … adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status … enforced boolean; and canonical target details.'
scenarios: Executor invents differing field names across CLI, MCP, and audit surfaces, creating a de-facto schema split that later callers must reconcile
UI rendering and audit serialization disagree on what 'canonical target details' contains, breaking AC 21 verification
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'canonical target details' with an explicit sub-schema, e.g.: 'canonical target details: a list of per-pin records each containing {canonical_id: string, role: child|prerequisite, pinned_fingerprint: string, current_fingerprint: string|null, pin_status: current|stale-pin-drift|stale-pin-missing|malformed-pin|legacy-unpinned}.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define all sub-fields of `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.canonical target details` (e.g., `canonical_id`, `role`, `pinned_fingerprint`, `current_fingerprint`) as a named schema co-located with the rest of the health contract.
evidence: Plan quote: 'canonical target details' — no sub-fields enumerated anywhere in the plan
AC: 'All detailed review surfaces consume the shared derived-health contract' — consumers cannot implement this without knowing the sub-field names
ABSENCE: No schema or field list for 'canonical target details' appears anywhere in the plan text
Searched codebase: no existing DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema found
findings.finding: DerivedPlanReviewHealth 'canonical target details' sub-schema is referenced but never defined. The plan names this field as part of the nested health contract but never enumerates its sub-fields (e.g., canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, current_material).
findings.location: Enforcement and rollout section: 'compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status ... and canonical target details.'
scenarios: An implementor building the close-error display or audit output doesn't know which fields to render — they must guess or use ad-hoc field names
UI/CLI surfaces each invent different field names, making the 'shared derived-health contract' non-shared in practice
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `TargetPinDetail` sub-schema inline in the 'Enforcement and rollout' section: `{canonical_id: str, role: 'child'|'prerequisite', pinned_fingerprint: str, current_fingerprint: str|None, drift_kind: pin_status_value}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the sort key for plan-material-pin role ordering (e.g., 'lexicographic: child before prerequisite') so that both the builder and any re-implementation produce an identical manifest.
evidence: Plan quote: 'zero or more plan-material-pin lines sorted by role then canonical ID'
Lexicographic sort puts 'child' before 'prerequisite' (c < p), but this is not confirmed as the intended order
ABSENCE: No explicit role enum sort order is defined anywhere in the plan
The existing manifest.py uses Python sorted() on path strings — the same approach would apply here, but the plan should confirm this for role ordering
findings.finding: The role sort order for plan-material-pin manifest lines ('sorted by role then canonical ID') is not defined. The two role values are 'child' and 'prerequisite'; whether the sort is lexicographic, or a defined enum order (child first vs prerequisite first), is unspecified.
findings.location: Signed plan-material pins section: 'zero or more plan-material-pin lines sorted by role then canonical ID'
scenarios: An implementor re-implementing the manifest builder from the spec and a verifier that reads it could produce different orderings if one uses enum-position order and the other uses lexicographic order, breaking signature reproduction
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'role sort is lexicographic (child < prerequisite); within a role, sort by canonical ID lexicographically' to the manifest order specification.

findings.finding: The 0.80 execution priority-floor applied to Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution is a behavioral change to existing plan-review evaluation that has no dedicated feature flag — it activates automatically for any in_progress ticket after deployment. The stated operational rollback ('disabling both enforcement flags') does NOT revert this threshold change; only a full code rollback does. The plan provides no intermediate mechanism (flag, config, or expand-contract step) to disable just the threshold change independently of the enforcement gates.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate: 'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add `verify.execution_priority_floor` config (default 0.80, settable to null/0.0 to disable) so operators can revert without a code rollback; or (b) explicitly state in the plan that the threshold change is irreversible short of a code rollback and add a numbered rollback runbook step to the 'Enforcement and rollout' section covering this case alongside the enforcement-flag steps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the rollback sequence to explicitly state that disabling the two opt-in gates does NOT suppress the 0.80 per-review execution floor and that only deploying the preceding code version removes it; separate the two rollback paths (gates vs floor) to avoid implying they are equivalent.
evidence: 'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version'
'During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80)'
'A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added'
findings.finding: Cross-section contradiction on the rollback path for the 0.80 execution-review floor: the Enforcement and rollout section instructs 'To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates [require_plan_review_for_close and enforce_plan_material_pins], then deploy the preceding code version,' but the Execution reviews section states the 0.80 floor is applied unconditionally whenever a ticket is in execution phase ('During execution, normal Pass 3 blocking-threshold resolution applies effective_block_threshold = max(..., 0.80)') and explicitly adds 'A runtime floor flag is intentionally not added.' Disabling the two opt-in gates does not suppress the floor during review; only rolling back the code does. The rollback instructions therefore partially contradict the unconditional-floor design.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout (rollback paths paragraph) vs Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate (0.80 floor paragraph)
scenarios: An operator disables both flags expecting the 0.80 floor to stop applying to in-progress tickets, but reviews of execution-phase tickets still apply the floor until the code is rolled back — creating a false sense of having completed rollback.
A future maintainer reads the rollback instructions and concludes disabling the gates is sufficient, misses the code-deploy step.
findings.suggested_fix: Split the rollback description into two clearly labelled paths: (1) 'To suppress close-gate and enforcement-only behaviour: set enforce_plan_material_pins=false and require_plan_review_for_close=false — the 0.80 execution floor continues to apply during reviews.' (2) 'To also remove the 0.80 execution-review floor: complete step 1 first, then deploy the preceding code version.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly verifies a current planning-phase review is accepted as 'compatible' for an execution-phase ticket when close enforcement is enabled (e.g., 'A valid planning-phase review satisfies the compatible-review requirement for an execution-phase ticket at close').
evidence: 'Execution mode accepts planning (the stricter full review) or execution with priority-floor numerically at least 0.80; lower or malformed execution floors are incompatible'
AC16: 'Enabled close enforcement requires a separately earned compatible review' — does not distinguish or test the planning→execution compatibility direction
Absence of any AC that verifies a planning-phase review is accepted when the ticket's phase is execution
findings.finding: Described deliverable 'planning-phase review satisfies an execution-phase ticket's close requirement' has no covering acceptance criterion. The Execution reviews section explicitly states 'Execution mode accepts planning (the stricter full review) or execution with priority-floor numerically at least 0.80,' meaning a planning review is a valid substitute for an execution review at close. No AC verifies this cross-phase compatibility path.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate ('Planning mode accepts only planning…' paragraph); Acceptance Criteria (AC16)
scenarios: An implementer only reads the ACs and implements execution-phase close to reject planning-phase reviews, since no AC demands the cross-phase acceptance path work.
A regression removes planning→execution acceptance and no AC catches it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering manifest parser error handling, e.g., 'Duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or out-of-range numeric manifest lines each cause the plan-review validity result to be malformed/invalid rather than silently accepted.'
evidence: 'New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines'
'manifest_pins() returns [] only when no pin lines exist; manifest_priority_floor() returns None only when no floor line exists'
'compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch and returns malformed/invalid'
No AC references parser error paths, ManifestFormatError, or the malformed/invalid return value explicitly
findings.finding: Described deliverable — new manifest parsers raising ManifestFormatError on duplicate, malformed, unknown-enum, missing-token, extra-token, or invalid numeric lines, and compute_plan_review_validity returning malformed/invalid on any such error — has no directly covering acceptance criterion. All ACs address higher-level consumer behaviour or specific field values, but none verifies that the parsers correctly detect and surface format errors.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins ('New parsers raise one ManifestFormatError…' paragraph and 'In the plan-review attestation branch, compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch…')
scenarios: A parser silently ignores a duplicate pin line (returning the first occurrence) instead of raising ManifestFormatError; no AC fails.
An invalid numeric floor is accepted without error; downstream enforcement logic never sees malformed/invalid.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the config parser tolerates unknown keys on rollback, or add an explicit config-cleanup step (remove the new keys before deploying the preceding code version) to the rollback path.
evidence: "To remove the 0.80 execution-review behavior, first disable both gates, then deploy the preceding code version"
No mention of config-parser leniency toward unknown keys, nor of a config-file cleanup step before rolling back code.
findings.finding: The rollback plan ('deploy the preceding code version') does not address whether the preceding code version will error on the new config keys `enforce_plan_material_pins` and `require_plan_review_for_close` if they have been written to a shared config file. If the old config parser rejects unknown keys, a rollback attempt would leave deployments in a broken state — an unacknowledged breakage in the rollback path.
findings.location: Settled design / Enforcement and rollout — rollback paths paragraph
scenarios: Operator adds `enforce_plan_material_pins = true` to config, then rolls back code; old binary errors on unknown config key and fails to start.
CI pipeline runs old code against a repo whose config file has already been updated with the new keys.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the rollback section: 'Before deploying the preceding code version, remove `enforce_plan_material_pins` and `require_plan_review_for_close` from all config files, or confirm that the preceding version's config parser silently ignores unknown keys.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit documentation target (e.g., config reference page or ADR section) for `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`, including defaults, scope, and interaction with close gates.
evidence: "verify.enforce_plan_material_pins defaults to false"
"verify.require_plan_review_for_close defaults to false"
Health and documentation section covers UI surfaces but does not mention a config reference or ops-guide entry for these keys.
findings.finding: The two new operator-facing config keys (`verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`) are described only in the design prose; no documentation target (user-facing config reference, ops guide, or ADR entry) is assigned for them.
findings.location: Settled design / Enforcement and rollout; Health and documentation section
scenarios: An operator enabling enforcement in a new project has no documented reference for what the keys do, their defaults, or their interaction with close gates.
A future operator reading the config reference finds the keys missing or stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` and the prerequisite coverage record schema to a named documentation target (e.g., extend the advanced attestation docs or add a schema reference), alongside the already-planned plan-material-pin documentation.
evidence: "Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI"
No corresponding doc/ADR assignment for DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract or prerequisite coverage record schema.
"plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation" — but DerivedPlanReviewHealth and prerequisite coverage schema are not similarly assigned.
findings.finding: The `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract (pin_status, phase_status, enforced, canonical target details) and the prerequisite coverage record schema (disposition values, prerequisite_id attribution, indeterminate reason_codes) are exposed on user-facing surfaces (detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, web UI, close errors) but no documentation target is named for either schema.
findings.location: Settled design / Health and documentation; Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: A tool author consuming the MCP ticket detail endpoint encounters the new health fields with no schema reference.
A reviewer reading CLI output sees new disposition values (consistent / finding / indeterminate) with no documentation explaining them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign manifest format documentation (covering the new `review-phase`, `priority-floor`, and `plan-material-pin` lines and the updated deterministic ordering) to the Health and documentation section or a named doc artifact.
evidence: "The final deterministic manifest order is: plan-review, ticket, material, model, runner, blocking, advisory, review-phase, optional priority-floor, rebar-version, optional regver, optional disabled_builtins, optional refreshed-from, optional verified-at-sha, zero or more plan-material-pin lines..."
Health and documentation section does not mention updating manifest format docs.
findings.finding: The plan adds new mandatory manifest line types (`review-phase`, `priority-floor`, `plan-material-pin`) with a revised deterministic ordering, but does not state that existing manifest format documentation will be updated to reflect the new line types and their ordering rules.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — manifest order paragraph; Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: A developer implementing a custom verifier follows the existing manifest format docs and produces signatures that fail because the ordering now includes new required line types.
An integration test author writing fixture manifests produces malformed manifests because the documented order is stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement spike or historical-fixture analysis that samples existing execution-phase review outcomes to validate that 0.80 is a defensible starting floor (e.g., measure what fraction of current execution reviews would be blocked/passed at 0.80 vs. neighboring thresholds).
evidence: "The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config."
No spike, pilot, or fixture measuring pass/block rates at this threshold is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
AC: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 priority-floor' — asserted, not measured.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-review priority floor is asserted as a protocol risk policy with no empirical validation, spike, or measurement plan. The plan explicitly disclaims it as an 'empirically claimed optimum,' but no fixture or pilot is defined to confirm that 0.80 is neither too restrictive (blocking legitimate execution-phase work) nor too permissive (failing to surface meaningful risks) in practice.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: The 0.80 floor blocks too many valid execution-phase reviews, causing friction without risk benefit.
The 0.80 floor is too low for the actual risk profile of in-progress work, defeating the policy intent.
A future floor change (plan mentions 'a future required floor') is made on equally unvalidated grounds.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small fixture set of representative execution-phase review traces (pass, borderline, fail), run them through the 0.80 gate before shipping, and record observed block/pass rates as the empirical baseline for future tuning. Document the baseline in the design notes so the 'future required floor' decision has data to reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task (or AC) to record the key architectural decisions — signed-pin model, natural propagation rationale, and pre-sign transaction protocol — in an ADR or AGENTS.md section before closing the epic.
evidence: "plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation." — only user-facing documentation is mentioned.
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design doc for the signed-pin architecture, propagation model, or pre-sign transaction protocol.
ABSENCE: no 'Architecture Decision Record' or equivalent is named anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — signed plan-material pins, the natural (non-eager) propagation model, the pre-sign transaction/revision-locking protocol, and the prerequisite consistency criterion as a specialized isolated pipeline stage — but specifies no update to an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design document to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation section — mentions only user-facing and API documentation, not architectural decision records.
scenarios: A future engineer re-evaluates whether eager propagation is better without knowing the tradeoffs documented here.
The pre-sign transaction/retry protocol is misunderstood and 'simplified' by removing the outside-lock retry loop.
The rationale for natural propagation vs. graph-wide invalidation is lost, leading to an inconsistent extension.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a new ADR (e.g., docs/adr/0NNN-plan-material-pins.md) covering: (1) signed pins vs. persisted invalidation events, (2) natural vs. eager propagation, (3) the pre-sign revision-lock protocol. Reference it from the advanced attestation documentation already planned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a post-replay (or end-of-reduce_ticket) bootstrap step for `plan_review_phase` that fires when the field is absent after replaying all events, mirroring how `creation_channel` re-infers from restored genesis fields and `managed_refs` seeds from current state in `process_snapshot` — this covers no-SNAPSHOT tickets, not just SNAPSHOT-bearing ones.
evidence: Plan states bootstrap fires in process_snapshot via 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction; the derived value is then carried forward idempotently in later snapshots.'

process_snapshot in _processors.py (lines 662–726) is the ONLY hook called for SNAPSHOT events; for no-snapshot tickets replay_events runs all events through _EVENT_HANDLERS without a post-replay bootstrap step.
Analogous projections (managed_refs, creation_channel) have BOTH a process_snapshot migration guard AND a per-event or end-of-replay derivation.
ABSENCE: the plan names no end-of-replay hook (e.g., in replay_events or reduce_ticket) that derives plan_review_phase when no SNAPSHOT is present.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the plan_review_phase bootstrap rule only within process_snapshot (for SNAPSHOT-bearing tickets), but does not specify where bootstrap fires during a full raw-event replay for tickets that have no SNAPSHOT in their event log. The plan relies on a 'reducer-cache version bump' to trigger re-reduction, but for a ticket that was never compacted (no SNAPSHOT event), process_snapshot never runs, leaving no defined hook to write the initial plan_review_phase value. The pattern for analogous projections (managed_refs seeds from create; authorship is counted per-event; last_reopened_at is set per STATUS) always has both a process_snapshot migration AND a per-event or end-of-replay initialization. Without the latter, tickets that have never been compacted may emerge from the first post-upgrade replay without plan_review_phase, causing unexpected planning/execution classification or downstream bootstrap failures until the ticket is compacted.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate — 'For a pre-feature SNAPSHOT missing plan_review_phase, bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status. A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'
scenarios: A newly-created ticket (only CREATE + STATUS events, no compaction yet) is reviewed post-upgrade; plan_review_phase is absent from compiled state, causing the review-phase parser to return 'planning' by default — acceptable only if the default is always correct, but the plan says 'execution' for in_progress tickets, which could be wrong here.

A ticket that has been in_progress for months without compaction returns None/absent for plan_review_phase on first post-upgrade reduction, triggering a bootstrap from compiled status — but the plan doesn't say this bootstrap occurs during replay, only during process_snapshot.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-replay derivation step (e.g. at the end of `replay_events` or in `reduce_ticket`) that, when `plan_review_phase` is absent from `state` after all events have been applied, bootstraps it from the computed `state['status']` using the same rule (open/idea → planning; other → execution). This is symmetric with how the plan describes the process_snapshot migration and ensures every ticket has a defined phase value after the first post-upgrade reduction regardless of whether it has been compacted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and explicitly justify the custom plan-material-pin manifest format against established dependency-attestation prior art (in-toto link attestations, SLSA provenance, SBOM pinning) — or confirm that the existing DSSE/op-cert infrastructure is already aligned with one of these standards.
evidence: 'The final deterministic manifest order is: ... zero or more plan-material-pin lines sorted by role then canonical ID'
'Signed pins extend the existing computed-on-read attestation-validity model: the downstream review cryptographically records exactly what related plan material it relied on'
ABSENCE: no mention of in-toto, SLSA provenance, SBOM (CycloneDX/SPDX), or any supply-chain attestation standard that solves exactly this problem
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 'signed plan-material pins in manifest text lines' attestation scheme that closely mirrors established prior art (in-toto link attestations, SLSA provenance records, supply-chain SBOM pinning) without acknowledging or distinguishing from those approaches. The plan is potentially reinventing a structured dependency-material attestation pattern that already has standardized formats and tooling.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins
scenarios: Reviewers or future maintainers unfamiliar with the custom pin format discover after the fact that in-toto link attestations already encode 'materials used' with similar semantics, creating a parallel ad-hoc standard inside the project.
A future integration with external tooling (e.g., SLSA verifiers) requires a separate translation layer because the internal format diverges from the established standard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a codebase citation or new regression test) that the existing DSSE/op-cert manifest verifier and all field-specific parsers tolerate unknown/unrecognized manifest lines without error or signature invalidation, and add an explicit AC covering this.
evidence: 'an older verifier authenticates that stored list including unknown pin lines, then its field-specific parsers ignore lines they do not consume'
'Therefore unknown additive lines do not alter signature reproduction'

ABSENCE: no codebase citation confirming the existing manifest verifier's behavior with unknown lines, and no AC line requiring a regression test that proves older verifiers accept pin-bearing attestations
findings.finding: The plan asserts backward compatibility rests on 'an older verifier authenticates that stored list including unknown pin lines, then its field-specific parsers ignore lines they do not consume' — but this is an unverified capability assertion about the existing DSSE/op-cert and manifest-parsing implementation. If the existing verifier or parsers do not already handle unknown lines gracefully (e.g., strict line enumeration, unknown-line rejection), the entire backward-compatibility guarantee collapses.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins — 'Backward verification does not reconstruct a manifest from recognized fields'
scenarios: Existing manifest parser uses an allowlist or strict exhaustion check — adding pin lines causes ManifestFormatError in older deployed verifiers, silently invalidating all new attestations when read by unupgraded consumers.
An audit or re-sign path that reconstructs the manifest from parsed fields silently drops pin lines, breaking the signature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A pin-bearing attestation read by a pre-pin verifier passes signature verification and all field-parsers return their defaults without error.' Ground this in a unit test against the existing parser code path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art acknowledgment in 'Why this approach' comparing the chosen single-hop lazy invalidation against transitive eager invalidation (as used in build systems), explicitly bounding the failure mode (silent multi-hop drift) and confirming it is acceptable given the expected dependency depth and usage patterns.
evidence: 'Propagation is deliberately natural rather than eager: an upstream change invalidates its direct dependent; farther downstream tickets invalidate only if the intermediate ticket adapts materially'
'This avoids tickets-branch write amplification, introduces no new storage primitive'
ABSENCE: no reference to build-system or reactive-graph literature that characterizes when lazy single-hop invalidation is preferable to transitive invalidation
findings.finding: The plan's 'natural/lazy propagation' model (upstream change invalidates only its direct dependent) is a deliberate architectural choice but is not compared against the well-studied build-system lazy-invalidation alternatives (Make/Ninja dependency tracking, Bazel action-graph staleness, reactive dependency DAGs) that handle exactly this tradeoff. The plan states the rationale but does not demonstrate awareness of prior art for the lazy-vs-eager invalidation design space.
findings.location: Outcome section / Why this approach
scenarios: A long dependency chain (A→B→C→D) where B's description changes but B's review is not re-run: C's attestation stays valid based on stale B material, D's attestation stays valid, yet A's downstream meaning has propagated silently — the exact risk the plan aims to prevent.
Reviewers challenge the 'natural propagation' choice without a documented comparison, leading to re-architecture mid-implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for plan_review_phase explicitly: either confirm the already-deployed old reducer already passes unknown snapshot keys through compaction unchanged (with a test citation), or add a compensating-forward note acknowledging that phase values in compacted snapshots written between upgrade and rollback may be re-bootstrapped from compiled status (and verify that re-bootstrap is always correct for those tickets).
evidence: 'an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it'
'Compatibility tests run old reduction plus re-compaction against a phase-bearing snapshot, then roll forward and prove the exact phase value survives'
Plan does NOT state whether the currently-deployed old code already has this property or whether it is being added as part of this epic's scope
Post-rollback state for already-written phase values in compacted snapshots is not explicitly asserted
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback safety for plan_review_phase by claiming 'an older reducer must pass plan_review_phase through unchanged on re-compaction rather than strip it,' but this is a behavioral requirement on the old (pre-rollback) code that the plan does not confirm is already satisfied. If the deployed old reducer's compaction strips unknown snapshot keys (a common default), rolling back would silently discard plan_review_phase from any snapshot it re-compacts, leaving those tickets in an incorrect phase state (execution-phase tickets re-bootstrapped as planning, or vice versa, depending on their compiled status). The plan mentions compatibility tests but does not assert what the post-rollback schema state of already-compacted snapshots will be for tickets that never get re-reduced after rollback.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout (rollback section); AC: 'The plan_review_phase projection survives compaction.'
scenarios: Operator rolls back after shipping; existing compacted snapshots containing plan_review_phase are re-compacted by the old reducer which strips unknown keys; execution-phase tickets now bootstrap incorrectly as planning (or vice versa) on next read, silently accepting reviews or blocking work that should not be blocked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'The old reducer's compaction must be confirmed (via existing compatibility test coverage or a new cross-version test) to pass unknown compiled-state keys through unchanged. If it does not, the rollback procedure must include a note that plan_review_phase in any snapshot compacted between upgrade and rollback will be re-derived from compiled status on next reduction — and document the cases where that re-derivation is incorrect (e.g., an in_progress ticket whose phase was reset to planning by an open event after bootstrap).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a named criterion ID for prerequisite consistency (e.g., `prereq-consistency`), specify its isolated prompt structure (system prompt posture + output schema fields including mandatory `prerequisite_id` attribution), and describe how it is registered in the criteria routing infrastructure.
evidence: Plan quote: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material.'
Plan quote: 'The criterion receives the subject plan and the current narrow material of each prerequisite; existing parent/child criteria and prompts are unchanged.'
ABSENCE: No criterion_id (like 'prereq-consistency' or similar) is ever assigned
ABSENCE: No prompt template, system prompt design, or output schema for the LLM call to this criterion is specified
The plan says 'isolated prompts' but does not define what isolation means structurally (separate system prompt? separate agent call? same runner?)
Existing pass1.py CONTAINER_CRITERIA pattern shows criteria are registered by ID — no new criterion ID is defined
findings.finding: The 'specialized criterion' for prerequisite consistency review has no prompt specification, no criterion_id, and no explicit mapping to the existing criteria routing infrastructure. The plan describes its inputs, output schema, and isolation guarantees but never names the criterion or specifies how its prompt is registered or invoked.
findings.location: Prerequisite consistency review section: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist... The criterion receives the subject plan and the current narrow material of each prerequisite'
scenarios: An implementor cannot write the criterion without knowing its ID (required to route findings back through Pass 2/3) or its prompt structure

The 'attribution' validation in Pass 2 requires the criterion's findings to carry a prerequisite_id — but the prompt must instruct the LLM to emit this field; without a prompt spec, attribution errors will be endemic

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit story dependency from 5967 to acaf and verify 5967's pre-sign transaction integration tests cover relationship and fingerprint change abort using a concrete PlanRelationSnapshot stub.
evidence: 5967 implementation: 'Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM call' — PlanRelationSnapshot is named but owned by acaf.
5967 AC: 'Unified pre-sign comparison aborts after any phase, own-material, relationship, or related-fingerprint change' — relationship and related-fingerprint require acaf's contract.
acaf implementation: 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision' — acaf owns the contract.
acaf: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection...The pin story does not acquire a separate sign lock' — confirms 5967 must integrate acaf's output but no explicit depends_on is stated.
findings.finding: Child 5967 owns the unified pre-sign transaction and its ACs require aborting after 'relationship or related-fingerprint change', but PlanRelationSnapshot — the contract that supplies those relationship and fingerprint dimensions — is defined and owned by child acaf. Child 5967's implementation section references PlanRelationSnapshot without naming acaf as a prerequisite, leaving the pre-sign relationship/fingerprint abort dimension unimplementable from 5967 alone. An integration that delivers 5967 before acaf will have a structurally incomplete pre-sign comparison for those two dimensions.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (pre-sign transaction) and child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (PlanRelationSnapshot definition)
scenarios: 5967 is implemented and shipped first as a standalone story; the pre-sign comparison covers phase and own material but silently omits relationship/fingerprint dimensions until acaf lands, leaving AC4 ('Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change') partially unimplemented in intermediate state.
A code reviewer reading only 5967 cannot identify what PlanRelationSnapshot contains or where the relationship/fingerprint signals come from.
findings.suggested_fix: Mark acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c as a prerequisite of 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, and add a joint integration test that exercises the unified pre-sign comparison with both phase-change and relationship-change trigger paths against the concrete PlanRelationSnapshot type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit story dependencies from 75de to 5967 and acaf, and add an AC to 75de specifying that the single ManifestFormatError catch covers manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), and manifest_pins() together.
evidence: Parent Settled Design: 'compute_plan_review_validity parses all three fields together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch and returns malformed/invalid.'
Parent: 'Add manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), and manifest_pins() parsers beside existing manifest parsers; every lifecycle consumer uses them.'
75de implementation: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' — does not enumerate which parsers are inside the catch.
5967 implementation: defines manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() with ManifestFormatError semantics.
acaf implementation: defines manifest_pins() with ManifestFormatError semantics.
75de has no explicit reference to 5967 or acaf as story dependencies.
findings.finding: Child 75de owns compute_plan_review_validity and the parent design specifies it must parse manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor() (from 5967), and manifest_pins() (from acaf) together inside one narrow ManifestFormatError catch. Child 75de's implementation section does not name 5967 or acaf as dependencies, nor does it describe the three-parser catch scope. A developer implementing 75de independently could implement the catch without the phase/floor parsers (5967) or the pins parser (acaf), leaving the catch scope inconsistent with the parent design.
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b (compute_plan_review_validity / ManifestFormatError catch)
scenarios: 75de is implemented first; its ManifestFormatError catch contains only legacy parser calls and does not yet call manifest_review_phase() or manifest_pins(); a malformed phase or pin line therefore goes uncaught and surfaces as an unhandled exception rather than a malformed-review verdict.
An implementer reading 75de in isolation adds a partial ManifestFormatError catch that covers pins but not phase/floor, or vice versa, creating an asymmetric malformed-review behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de's implementation section, enumerate the three parsers called inside the ManifestFormatError catch and add a test fixture covering all three error paths (malformed phase, malformed floor, malformed pin) as a single catch scenario.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit story dependency from 7c27 to 5967 and confirm 7c27's Pass-3 blocking-threshold tests import and call the shared function defined in 5967 rather than a local duplicate.
evidence: 7c27 implementation: 'Reuse the phase story's threshold function; do not add a separate decide pipeline.'
5967 implementation: 'normal Pass-3 resolution uses effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) for blocking criteria.'
7c27 has no explicit depends_on or prerequisite reference to 5967.
findings.finding: Child 7c27 says 'Reuse the phase story's threshold function' — meaning the max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) blocking-threshold logic — without naming child 5967 as a prerequisite. This threshold function is defined and owned by 5967 ('effective_block_threshold = max(registry.threshold_for(criterion), 0.80) to blocking criteria'). If 7c27 is implemented before 5967, the prerequisite criterion would either duplicate or stub the threshold function, creating a divergence from the canonical implementation.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (threshold function reuse) and child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (threshold function owner)
scenarios: 7c27 is implemented before 5967; the developer writes a local copy of the threshold function in the prerequisite criterion rather than reusing 5967's canonical one, creating two implementations that can diverge when the policy value changes.
The threshold function changes in a future policy revision; only 5967's version is updated, while 7c27's reuse silently points to an older copy if the dependency was not formally tracked.
findings.suggested_fix: In 7c27's implementation section, add 'depends on 5967 for the shared effective_block_threshold function' and add a cross-module import assertion in the test suite to prevent local duplication.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
